extension.valueMarkdown:
## Character
6-year-old Indian-American girl. Lives with her mother Priya Patel (age 34, software engineer) and father Raj Patel (age 36, pharmacist) in Raleigh, NC. Attends first grade. Generally active and happy but has significant allergic disease burden.

## Clinical Scenario
Aisha was diagnosed with asthma at age 3 after recurrent wheezing with viral URIs. She progressed to severe persistent asthma by age 5, requiring daily fluticasone/salmeterol plus montelukast, with PRN albuterol. She has confirmed IgE-mediated food allergies to peanuts (anaphylaxis at age 2 — epinephrine auto-injector prescribed) and tree nuts (positive skin prick and specific IgE testing). She also has moderate atopic dermatitis managed with topical steroids and moisturizers, and allergic rhinitis.

Her care involves a pediatrician for routine well-child visits and acute illness, a pediatric allergist/immunologist for allergy management and immunotherapy evaluation, and she has had two ED visits at a children's hospital — one for an anaphylactic reaction at age 2 (accidental peanut exposure at a family gathering) and one for a severe asthma exacerbation at age 5 requiring systemic steroids.

Priya Patel, as Aisha's mother, is the authorized representative who uses a patient-facing app to manage Aisha's health records.

## Sites
- **Triangle Pediatrics** — Pediatrician in Raleigh, NC (2020–present). Well-child visits, immunizations, acute visits, asthma management.
- **UNC Pediatric Allergy & Immunology** — Allergist in Chapel Hill, NC (2022–present). Food allergy testing, asthma specialist management, immunotherapy evaluation.
- **WakeMed Children's Emergency Department** — ED in Raleigh, NC. Two visits: anaphylaxis (2022) and severe asthma exacerbation (2025).

Three sites, one state (NC). Tests Permission Ticket constraints primarily on **scopes** (e.g., can filter to just immunization records, or just allergy-related data) and **organizations** (3 different health systems).

note.text: Referral to Dr. Krishnamurthy at UNC Pediatric Allergy and Immunology for FeNO testing and spirometry evaluation given step-up in asthma therapy from low-dose ICS to ICS/LABA combination.

code.text: Re-referral to Dr. Krishnamurthy at UNC Pediatric Allergy and Immunology for asthma severity reassessment and consideration of add-on biologic therapy (omalizumab)

note.text: Re-referral to Dr. Sunita Krishnamurthy at UNC Pediatric Allergy and Immunology for asthma severity reassessment and consideration of add-on biologic therapy (omalizumab) given reclassification to severe persistent asthma despite step-up to ICS/LABA combination therapy.

resource.description: Written asthma action plan reviewed with caregiver and provided as PDF for family and daycare. Green zone: fluticasone BID, no rescue inhaler needed. Yellow zone: albuterol every 4 hours, call office if >3 uses in 24 hours. Red zone: albuterol every 20 minutes x3, call 911 if no improvement.

Revised asthma action plan reflecting step-up to fluticasone/salmeterol (Advair HFA 45/21) with spacer mouthpiece, addition of montelukast 4 mg daily, continued albuterol PRN rescue therapy, and updated green/yellow/red zone criteria.

Updated asthma action plan reflecting the change from Advair HFA 45/21 to Advair HFA 115/21 as the daily controller medication, with green/yellow/red zone instructions and emergency guidance for severe persistent asthma classification.

attachment.title: Updated Asthma Action Plan — Advair HFA 115/21

resource.description: Updated 504 plan documentation and school nurse notification reflecting the change in asthma classification to severe persistent and the new controller medication (Advair HFA 115/21).

attachment.title: Updated 504 Plan and School Health Forms — Severe Persistent Asthma

resource.description: Updated 504 plan documentation prepared for kindergarten entry, accommodating severe persistent asthma (rescue inhaler access, activity modifications) and food allergies (peanut and tree nut avoidance, anaphylaxis action plan, EpiPen availability at school).

type.text: 504 Plan Renewal and Asthma Action Plan for School

resource.description: Updated 504 plan renewal documentation and updated asthma action plan for provision to the school nurse, documenting current asthma medications, rescue inhaler instructions, epinephrine auto-injector availability, and activity modifications.

attachment.title: 504 Plan Renewal and Asthma Action Plan — First Grade

note.text: Peanut allergy confirmed after anaphylactic reaction on 10/22/2022 at WakeMed ED. Strict peanut and tree nut avoidance advised pending allergist evaluation. EpiPen Jr 0.15 mg prescribed, two-pack carried at all times.

Third wheezing episode in 8 weeks. Prior episodes managed with PRN albuterol; this episode more severe with incomplete response to home albuterol and overnight symptoms. Likely trajectory toward persistent asthma diagnosis; formal diagnosis deferred to upcoming 3-year well-child visit.
2023-03-15: Reclassified as mild persistent asthma (J45.30) at 3-year well-child visit. Condition superseded by formal asthma diagnosis.
Low-grade fever, rhinorrhea, pharyngeal erythema. Viral trigger for current wheezing exacerbation.

Reclassified from moderate persistent to severe persistent at post-ED follow-up visit (January 2025). Despite adherence to Advair HFA 45/21 BID and montelukast 4 mg daily, patient had an ED-level exacerbation requiring IV steroids and continuous nebulization, baseline rescue inhaler use 3-4 times/week, frequent nighttime symptoms, and multiple school absences. These findings meet NAEPP criteria for severe persistent asthma. ICS/LABA stepped up from Advair HFA 45/21 to Advair HFA 115/21.

5-year well-child visit: Improved on current step-up regimen (Advair HFA 115/21). Rescue inhaler use decreased to approximately once per week. Nighttime symptoms rare (approximately once per month). No ED visits or hospitalizations since January 2025 exacerbation. No school absences for respiratory symptoms in past 6 weeks. No step-down warranted at this time given recent exacerbation history and only two months on current regimen.

Acute severe asthma exacerbation triggered by viral URI. Treated at WakeMed Children's ED with IV steroids and continuous nebulization. Currently on day 3 of 5-day prednisolone burst. Clinically improved — SpO2 97% on room air, residual end-expiratory wheezes present but no distress.
Controlled on cetirizine 5 mg daily. Mild seasonal exacerbation expected with spring pollen.
Well controlled. Mild dry patches on bilateral forearms only, no active excoriation or lichenification. Continue ceramide moisturizer daily, triamcinolone 0.1% ointment PRN for flares.

extension.valueMarkdown: Dr. Harlow called Priya Patel six days after Aisha's ED visit for peanut anaphylaxis at WakeMed.

coding.display: Office visit for pediatric care and assessment (procedure)

extension.valueMarkdown: Aisha, age 2 years 11 months, was brought in by Priya for cough and wheezing of 2 days' duration in the setting of a runny nose and low-grade fever for 4 days. This was Aisha's third wheezing episode associated with a viral URI since December 2022.

reasonCode.text: 3-year well-child examination, persistent asthma diagnosed, inhaled corticosteroid therapy initiated

extension.valueMarkdown: Aisha presented for her 3-year well-child visit. Priya reported that since the February acute visit, Aisha had continued to have intermittent cough and wheeze, particularly at night 2–3 times per week and with outdoor play.

reasonCode.text: 4-year well-child examination, asthma step-up evaluation, immunizations

extension.valueMarkdown: Aisha presented for her 4-year well-child visit. Priya reported that asthma control had been suboptimal over the past several months despite adherence to fluticasone 44 mcg BID.

coding.display: Office visit for pediatric care and assessment (procedure)

reasonCode.text: Post-ED follow-up, asthma reclassified severe persistent

extension.valueMarkdown: Aisha, age 4 years 10 months, was seen 2 days after her ED discharge from WakeMed for acute severe asthma exacerbation. Priya reported Aisha had improved since discharge — she was completing the prednisolone course (day 3 of 5), cough was less frequent, and she had not had significant wheezing since leaving the ED.
Dr. Harlow's office called Priya for a one-week post-ED check-in.

Aisha presented for her 5-year well-child visit, two months after her severe asthma exacerbation. Priya reported that on the higher-dose Advair 115/21, asthma control had been significantly better — rescue inhaler use was down to about once per week, nighttime symptoms were rare (approximately once per month), and Aisha had not missed any school for respiratory symptoms in the past 6 weeks.
Routine fall telephone check-in for asthma management. Priya reported Aisha was doing well overall — she had started kindergarten in August and was adjusting well.

coding.display: Administration of vaccine to produce active immunity (procedure)

extension.valueMarkdown: Aisha presented for her annual influenza vaccination. No acute complaints.
Aisha presented for her 6-year well-child visit. She was thriving in first grade, performing well academically, and continued to enjoy drawing and swimming.

dosageInstruction.text: Prednisolone 12 mg (1 mg/kg/day) by mouth once daily for 3 days
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Give 4 mL (12 mg) by mouth once daily for 3 days.

coding.display: fluticasone propionate 44 MCG/INHAL Metered Dose Inhaler, 120 Actuations

dosageInstruction.text: 1 puff inhaled twice daily via spacer with mask
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Inhale 1 puff (44 mcg) twice daily using spacer with mask.

note.text: Discontinued 2024-03-15. Low-dose ICS monotherapy insufficient for moderate persistent asthma control. Replaced with fluticasone/salmeterol (Advair HFA 45/21) combination therapy.

coding.display: fluticasone propionate/salmeterol 45/21 MCG/INHAL Metered Dose Inhaler, 60 Actuations

dosageInstruction.text: 1 puff inhaled twice daily via spacer with mouthpiece
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Inhale 1 puff twice daily using spacer with mouthpiece.

note.text: Step-up from low-dose ICS monotherapy to medium-dose ICS/LABA combination for moderate persistent asthma. Transitioned from mask to mouthpiece as patient can now coordinate inhalation technique.
Stopped 2025-01-16: Dose inadequate for severe persistent asthma. Stepped up to Advair HFA 115/21.

dosageInstruction.text: Take 1 tablet (5 mg) by mouth once daily
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Give 1 tablet (5 mg) by mouth once daily.

note.text: Dose increased from 2.5 mg to 5 mg daily based on current weight (16.2 kg) and ongoing allergic rhinitis symptoms.

dosageInstruction.text: Inject 0.15 mg intramuscularly into outer thigh as needed for anaphylaxis
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Use as directed for signs of anaphylaxis. Inject into outer thigh. Call 911 immediately after use.

note.text: Prescription renewed at 4-year well-child visit. Indications for use and anaphylaxis emergency action plan reviewed with mother.

dosageInstruction.text: Chew 1 tablet (4 mg) once daily at bedtime
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Chew 1 tablet once daily at bedtime.

note.text: Add-on controller therapy for moderate persistent asthma, in addition to ICS/LABA combination.

coding.display: ADVAIR HFA 115/21 MCG/INHAL Metered Dose Inhaler, 60 Actuations

dosageInstruction.text: Inhale 1 puff by mouth twice daily using spacer with mask
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Inhale 1 puff by mouth twice daily using spacer with mask.

note.text: Step-up from Advair HFA 45/21 to Advair HFA 115/21 for severe persistent asthma. Asthma reclassified from moderate persistent after ED-level exacerbation despite adherence to prior regimen. Spacer technique reviewed with mother — good technique demonstrated.

note.text: Prescription renewed at 5-year well-child visit. Ensure school has current anaphylaxis action plan on file for kindergarten entry.

note.text: Prescription renewed at 6-year well-child visit. EpiPen Jr available at school with school nurse per 504 plan.
VIS provided to mother and reviewed. Consent obtained. 15-minute post-vaccination observation completed without adverse reaction.
